Glossary About
Renaissance & Late Scholasticism

Pietro Pomponazzi

1462 – 1525

An Aristotelian pressing the mortality of the soul to the edge of orthodoxy.

Pietro Pomponazzi’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as an Aristotelian natural philosophy of embodied cognition placed inside a larger hierarchy of minds: animal souls, human rational souls, celestial intelligences, angelic or demonic minds as treated by theology and Platonism, and finally the mind of God. His central claim is not that mind is “nothing but matter” in a modern reductionist sense. It is that the specifically human mind is the mind of a living, sensing, imagining, temporally located animal, and that its intellectual operations never appear in nature without bodily and imaginative conditions. The human intellect has a genuine dignity and a qualified immateriality, but not the absolute separability of God or the celestial intelligences.

The core formula is this: the human soul is mortal absolutely, immortal only in a qualified sense. It is “immortal” insofar as rational activity rises above mere organ-bound sensation: the intellect knows universals, reasons, deliberates, and shares imperfectly in the order of divine and celestial intellect. But it is mortal “simply” because it never exercises this power without phantasms, images, sensory materials, temporal succession, and the living body.

This makes Pomponazzi neither a straightforward medieval dualist nor a modern materialist. He is better described as a hylomorphic naturalist: the soul is the form of the body; the human mind is a power of that ensouled body; cognition is structured by the body’s sensory and imaginative life; and the possibility of purely separated intellect belongs not to human beings naturally, but to God and the celestial intelligences.

The theological and angelological material is therefore not peripheral. Pomponazzi’s account of human mind depends on a contrast with separated intellects. To understand what the human mind is, he asks what it is not: it is not God’s pure intellectual actuality, not the intuitive cognition of celestial intelligences, and not an angelic or demonic mind capable of operating without the sensory-imaginative basis that human beings require. That contrast is one of the deepest structural features of his philosophy of mind.

2. Method: “within natural limits”

Pomponazzi’s most famous treatment of the soul appears in De immortalitate animae of 1516, written after the Fifth Lateran Council’s intervention against doctrines denying the soul’s immortality or asserting a single intellect for all human beings. The work caused controversy and was attacked by ecclesiastical and philosophical critics, but Pomponazzi repeatedly frames his argument as a natural-philosophical inquiry, not as a theological denial of Christian doctrine.

The key methodological phrase is that one must reason “setting aside revelations and miracles” and remain within the limits of nature. In the proemium of De immortalitate, the question is explicitly posed in those terms: what can be said by natural reason, and what did Aristotle hold, if one brackets revealed authority?

This bracketing is crucial. Pomponazzi does not say that revelation is worthless. On the contrary, he says that Scripture and faith are to be preferred to merely human reasoning and experience. But as an Aristotelian natural philosopher, he asks what can be concluded from nature, experience, and the Peripatetic tradition alone.

That produces a two-level discourse. At the level of Christian doctrine, the immortality of the soul is affirmed. At the level of Aristotelian natural philosophy, Pomponazzi argues that the immortality of the individual human soul cannot be demonstrated and is, on Aristotelian premises, not the natural conclusion. This is not exactly the crude “double truth” caricature sometimes attached to Renaissance Aristotelianism. It is rather a disciplined separation of explanatory regimes: theology speaks from revelation; natural philosophy speaks from experience, causal explanation, and the interpretation of Aristotle.

3. The ontology of the soul: a unified, embodied form

Pomponazzi begins from the Aristotelian definition of soul as the form of a living body. The human soul is not a separate substance accidentally attached to a body; it is the organizing principle of the living human organism. This means that the soul is internally complex: vegetative, sensitive, and intellective powers are not three detachable souls, but powers of one unified human soul.

The vegetative powers concern nutrition, growth, and reproduction. The sensitive powers concern sensation, appetite, imagination, and the bodily-affective life shared with animals. The intellective powers concern universal cognition, reasoning, and willing. The question is whether the intellective power gives the human soul a separable, individually immortal existence after bodily death. Pomponazzi’s answer, as a natural philosopher, is no.

His argument turns on the distinction between operating through an organ and depending on the body for objects. Sensation operates through bodily organs: sight through eyes, hearing through ears, and so on. Intellection does not operate through a bodily organ in the same direct way. There is no “organ of universal thought” analogous to the eye for color. This gives the intellect a certain elevation above the sensitive powers. But the intellect still depends on the body because it depends on phantasms: images or imaginative presentations derived from sensory experience.

This is the decisive move. The human intellect is not organ-bound in the same manner as the senses, but it is still object-bound to bodily cognition. It cannot think without sensory-imaginative content. Therefore, it is not a self-subsisting intellectual substance. Its activity depends on the living psycho-physical unity of the human being.

Italian scholarship, especially the Treccani account associated with Umberto Eco’s encyclopedia project, captures this well: Pomponazzi treats the human being as an indissoluble psycho-physical unity and denies that natural reason shows the intellective soul surviving when its capacities never operate independently of the body.

4. Against Averroes and Aquinas

Pomponazzi’s position is constructed against two major alternatives: Averroism and Thomism.

Against the Averroist thesis of a single separate intellect shared by all human beings, Pomponazzi insists on the unity of the individual human soul. The human being thinks, not a detached universal intellect thinking through human beings as instruments. The Averroist view threatens the individuality of mental life and, in Pomponazzi’s eyes, produces an unacceptable rupture between the living human subject and the act of understanding. SEP notes that he called the Averroist unicity thesis “most false and unintelligible.”

Against Aquinas, however, Pomponazzi rejects the idea that the individual human soul is a subsistent immaterial form naturally capable of surviving bodily death. Thomism tries to preserve both embodiment and individual immortality: the soul is the form of the body, but also an intellectual substance that survives separation. Pomponazzi thinks this exceeds what natural Aristotelian philosophy can justify. If the human intellect always needs phantasms, then a separated human soul would either have to acquire a radically new mode of cognition after death or become comparable to celestial intelligences. Pomponazzi regards this as philosophically unstable within Aristotelian naturalism.

This is why he can reject Averroes without becoming a Thomist. He wants an individual human soul, but not an individually immortal one on natural grounds. The mind belongs to the individual human being precisely because it belongs to the living human organism.

5. The phantasm condition: no human thought without image

The deepest psychological thesis in Pomponazzi is the phantasm condition. Human beings think universally, but they do so through images. We do not encounter pure essences in the manner of a divine or angelic intellect. We arrive at universals from singulars: from sensed, remembered, imagined, and compared particulars.

Pomponazzi’s reasoning is empirical as well as textual. In experience, human thought is always accompanied by imaginative presentation. Damage to the sensory-imaginative basis of cognition impairs intellectual functioning. We do not observe a natural human act of thinking wholly free from images, time, attention, memory, and bodily condition.

This produces a strong theory of embodied cognition. Sensation is not merely a trigger that the intellect can leave behind. Imagination is not a dispensable ornament. Phantasms are constitutive conditions of human thought. To think “triangle,” “justice,” “animal,” or “God,” the human mind must work from images, signs, memories, examples, analogies, words, or internally represented particulars. The universal is grasped only through a relation to the singular.

In modern terms, Pomponazzi’s view resembles neither pure empiricism nor pure rationalism. It is not pure empiricism because the intellect does genuinely universalize; it is not mere sensation. But it is not rationalism because the intellect has no autonomous access to pure forms independent of the sensory-imaginative matrix. The human mind is rational, but its rationality is incarnate.

6. Qualified immateriality: why the human mind is not just another sense power

Pomponazzi does not collapse intellect into sensation. He repeatedly marks the human being as intermediate between mortal animals and immortal intelligences. The human soul is the noblest of material forms, near the boundary of immateriality. Spanish scholarship on Pomponazzi emphasizes this liminal status: the human soul “smells” of immateriality, but does not possess the absolute independence of a separated substance.

This means that the human mind has a dual character.

First, it is material in its dependence: it depends on phantasms, bodily temperament, the sensory apparatus, memory, time, and affective disposition.

Second, it is immaterial in its formal reach: it knows universals, judges, reasons, and directs itself toward truth and the good.

Pomponazzi’s subtlety lies in refusing to let the second point erase the first. The intellect’s universal object does not prove that the soul is a separable substance. It proves only that the human being occupies a high place in the hierarchy of natural forms.

This gives us a graded ontology of cognition. Plants live without sensing. Animals sense and imagine without universal intellection. Human beings sense, imagine, and reason. Celestial intelligences understand without sensory dependence. God understands without dependence of any kind. Human mind is therefore not the top of the hierarchy, but the highest embodied form.

7. Agent intellect: the most difficult point in the system

The most technically difficult part of Pomponazzi’s psychology concerns the agent intellect. In Aristotelian tradition, the agent intellect is the power by which potentially intelligible forms become actually intelligible. The problem is whether this agent intellect is part of the human soul, a separate intelligence, God, or some other external intellectual principle.

Pomponazzi’s treatment is notoriously unstable. Spanish scholarship by García-Valverde highlights this as one of the weak points of De immortalitate: Pomponazzi wants to preserve the mortality of the human soul, so he cannot make the agent intellect an immortal part of the individual human being. He therefore tends to detach the agent intellect from the human soul and associate it with a separated intelligence, perhaps the lowest celestial intelligence or even God in some interpretive contexts.

This creates a problem. If the agent intellect is not part of us, how do we abstract intelligible forms? Pomponazzi’s answer is that abstraction is in our power in a qualified way: we apply ourselves to abstraction, but the deeper actualizing principle is not simply identical with our individual soul. García-Valverde compares Pomponazzi’s explanation to the analogy of warming oneself by applying oneself to fire: the person does something voluntarily, but the causal power of heat belongs to the fire.

This is philosophically significant. It means that Pomponazzi’s account of human cognition is not enclosed inside the individual organism. Human thought depends internally on phantasms and externally on the cosmic-intellectual order that makes abstraction possible. The human mind is embodied, but it is not isolated. It is a node in a hierarchy of intelligibility extending upward toward separated intellects and God.

This is also where Pomponazzi’s philosophy of mind is most vulnerable. If the agent intellect is external, how exactly does it cooperate with the possible intellect in the human soul? If abstraction is in our power, why is speculative perfection rare? If the same external agent intellect is available to all, why are some humans philosophically gifted and others not? Spanish and Italian discussions emphasize that Pomponazzi can describe the passage from image to concept, but he has difficulty explaining the metaphysical mechanism that makes it possible.

8. Self-knowledge and the “flying man” problem

Pomponazzi also has implications for self-awareness. Avicenna’s famous “flying man” thought experiment suggests that a person suspended without sensory input would still affirm his own existence, implying a self-aware immaterial soul. Pomponazzi’s position pushes in the opposite direction: the human mind does not possess natural self-consciousness wholly independent of sensation and imagination.

This does not mean that Pomponazzi denies self-awareness. It means that self-awareness is embodied and mediated. We know ourselves through acts, memories, bodily affections, images, intentions, words, and relations to objects. A disembodied soul stripped of all sensory-imaginative content would not, on his Aristotelian naturalism, have the same mode of self-presence that theologians ascribe to separated souls or that Avicenna’s thought experiment suggests.

Here again, the contrast with God and separated intelligences matters. Divine self-knowledge is immediate; angelic or celestial intellectual self-knowledge is not mediated by phantasms in the human way. Human self-knowledge is reflexive, but not purely intellectual in the separated sense.

9. Individuation, personal identity, and death

Pomponazzi’s mortality thesis also has consequences for personal identity. If the soul is the form of the body, then the individual human mind is individuated by the individual living body. The destruction of that organism is not merely the loss of an instrument; it is the dissolution of the subject whose form the soul was. Spanish scholarship makes the point sharply: from a strictly Aristotelian standpoint, the human being is a matter-form composite, and when the composite dissolves, the subject disappears.

Pomponazzi argues that postmortem survival raises severe natural-philosophical problems. If individual souls survive, where are they? Are they in place or outside place? If the world is eternal, are there infinitely many separated souls? Does one need transmigration? If God creates each soul separately, is this a natural-philosophical explanation or a theological one? Pomponazzi regards such moves as unavailable within strict Aristotelian physics.

This gives his philosophy of mind a strong anti-Cartesian tendency before Descartes. There is no independently individuated mental substance that merely uses the body. The person is the living psycho-physical composite. The mind is not detachable without changing the very mode of cognition and subjectivity in question.

10. Human beings in the scale of minds

Pomponazzi’s anthropology is hierarchical. Human beings are intermediate. They are above animals because they possess intellect and will; they are below separated intelligences because their intellectual acts are discursive, temporal, image-dependent, and obscure.

A useful reconstruction of the hierarchy looks like this:

Kind of being Mode of cognition Dependence on body/phantasms Status
Plants Life without sensation Complete bodily dependence Mortal natural forms
Animals Sensation, appetite, imagination Complete sensory-organic dependence Mortal sensitive souls
Ordinary humans Sensation, imagination, practical reason, limited abstraction Phantasm-dependent Mortal absolutely, immortal qualifiedly
Philosophers or speculative humans Higher rational contemplation, still through phantasms Still embodied and temporal Highest human cognitive form
Celestial intelligences Intuitive intellectual cognition No phantasm dependence Separated intellectual substances
God Pure, independent, providential intellect No dependence Absolute source and order of intelligibility

The crucial point is that Pomponazzi’s philosophy of mind is not flat. It does not say, “all mind is embodied.” It says, more precisely: human mind is embodied; separated mind is possible at the divine and celestial levels; the error is to attribute that separated mode to the individual human soul on natural Aristotelian grounds.

11. God’s mind: not a distraction, but the upper limit of the theory

Pomponazzi’s God is not merely a theological afterthought. God functions as the ultimate efficient and final cause of the universe, the source of cosmic order, and the highest term in the hierarchy of intellect. In De fato, God’s will, providence, omnipotence, and foreknowledge are central to the problem of determinism and freedom.

God’s mind differs from human mind in at least four decisive ways.

First, God does not know through phantasms. Divine knowledge is not sensory, imaginative, temporal, or discursive.

Second, God knows essences and orders the cosmos. SEP’s account of De incantationibus notes that Pomponazzi presents God as knowing the essences of all species and arranging the universe through celestial intelligences.

Third, God’s intellect is inseparable from providence. The order of nature is not meaningless mechanism. Even when Pomponazzi naturalizes prodigies, illnesses, occult qualities, or religious cycles, he can still interpret them within an ordered universe governed by divine providence.

Fourth, God’s relation to freedom creates a tension inside the philosophy of mind. In the more deterministic strand of De fato, human will appears dependent on God’s will and celestial motions, with deliberation sometimes treated as an appearance generated by ignorance of causes. In the later theological strand, Pomponazzi explores a solution in which God limits divine omnipotence or foreknowledge in order to allow a form of contingency and negative liberty. Italian discussion of De fato emphasizes this oscillation between Stoic necessity and a more theologically inflected account of liberty.

So God’s mind is indispensable to the system. God is the paradigm of non-embodied intellect and the metaphysical ground of cosmic intelligibility. Human mind is understood partly by contrast with God’s mind: where God knows immediately, the human being reasons through images; where God orders universally, the human being deliberates under partial knowledge; where God’s intellect is pure actuality, human intellect is potential, intermittent, and materially conditioned.

12. Celestial intelligences, angels, and demons

Pomponazzi’s treatment of angels and demons must be handled carefully. He does not simply dismiss them as childish or irrelevant. Rather, he distinguishes several registers: Christian theological doctrine, Platonic or popular demonology, and Aristotelian natural philosophy.

In Christian and many non-Christian religious traditions, extraordinary events are often attributed to God, angels, or demons. Pomponazzi acknowledges this explanatory pattern. In the relevant primary passages, he notes that Christians and many “laws” or religious traditions posit such phenomena as coming from God or through ministering spirits, good or bad.

But as a Peripatetic philosopher, Pomponazzi asks whether such beings can play the causal and cognitive roles assigned to them. His objections are not merely skeptical; they are grounded in a theory of separated mind.

If demons or angels are immaterial, then they do not have sense organs. Without sense organs, how do they know particulars? They may know universals or essences, but particular contingent events require either sensory access or some other special mode of divine-like knowledge. If they use phantasms and sensation, then they are not purely immaterial; they would be corporeal and subject to generation and corruption.

Similarly, if demons can affect bodies, how do they do so? Pomponazzi argues, using a line also associated with Augustine in later discussion, that such beings may be said to move bodies but not to alter them immediately. If they require material intermediaries, then many alleged demonic effects can be explained more economically by natural causes, celestial influences, occult properties, bodily temperaments, vapors, and imagination.

This does not make angelic or demonic mind irrelevant. It makes it theoretically delicate. Pomponazzi is effectively asking: what kind of cognition would a separated mind have, and what kind of causal access to bodies could it possess? Those are philosophy-of-mind questions. His answer is that the only separated intellects securely admissible in Aristotelian natural philosophy are the celestial intelligences associated with the motions of the heavens. Ordinary demonological explanations multiply entities beyond what natural explanation requires.

The celestial intelligences, by contrast, are central. They are genuine separated intellects in the Peripatetic cosmos. They move the heavenly spheres and mediate cosmic order. Their cognition is not phantasm-dependent. They are therefore the nearest non-divine model of a purely intellectual mind.

13. Imagination, affect, and psychosomatic causality

Pomponazzi’s De incantationibus greatly expands the psychological side of his naturalism. He tries to explain many allegedly magical or demonic phenomena through natural causes, including celestial influence, occult qualities, bodily temperament, imagination, emotion, and the movement of spirits and blood.

This gives him a rich theory of psychosomatic causality. The imagination can affect the body. Strong passions alter complexion, pulse, heat, facial expression, voice, and bodily movement. A mind intensely fixed on an object may produce bodily effects through the mediation of spirits and humors. Pomponazzi even allows that collective imagination may contribute to effects involving vapors and clouds, though always under the broader order of nature and providence.

This is not modern psychology, but it is not superstition either. It is a Renaissance Aristotelian attempt to explain how mental, affective, and bodily processes interpenetrate. The imagination is not merely representational; it has causal power within the organism. Emotion is not an inner private state detached from physiology; it is a bodily-psychic event.

French scholarship on De incantationibus emphasizes this naturalistic etiology: Pomponazzi explains wonderful or apparently miraculous effects through strictly natural causes rather than by immediate supernatural intervention, while still leaving room for certain events that he does not simply reduce.

For a philosophy of mind, this is important. Pomponazzi’s mind is not a sealed rational faculty. It is entangled with imagination, affect, temperament, social expectation, celestial condition, and bodily susceptibility. Human rationality exists inside a psychophysiological ecology.

14. Will, freedom, and determinism

Pomponazzi’s philosophy of mind also includes a theory of will. In De fato, he confronts the relation between divine providence, celestial causality, fate, contingency, and human freedom. In one strand, he moves toward a strong determinism: God is the first efficient cause; celestial bodies are instruments; many events that appear accidental are necessary relative to hidden causes; human will itself depends on God’s will and celestial motions.

At this level, deliberation can appear as a phenomenon of partial ignorance. We experience ourselves as choosing because we do not grasp the full causal order determining appetite, imagination, temperament, judgment, and volition. This is not a crude denial that willing occurs. It is a denial that willing is an uncaused power floating free of nature.

The later theological treatment complicates the picture. Pomponazzi considers whether God may self-limit divine power or foreknowledge, thereby making room for contingency. He also explores a notion of liberty as negative: the will and intellect can suspend or withhold assent rather than simply produce an unconditioned positive act.

The resulting philosophy of mind is compatibilist or semi-compatibilist in tendency, though not neatly so. Human volition is cognitive-appetitive, embodied, temperamentally conditioned, and cosmically situated. Freedom is not sovereign independence from causality. It is, at most, a rational capacity for suspension, practical judgment, and self-regulation within an ordered causal world.

15. Ethics without natural immortality

Pomponazzi’s denial of naturally demonstrable immortality forces him to relocate ethics. If virtue is practiced only for postmortem reward, then virtue becomes mercenary. True virtue must be loved for itself. He argues that no good action is essentially unrewarded and no evil action essentially unpunished because virtue itself is the reward of virtue, and vice itself is the punishment of vice.

This is one of his most powerful philosophical moves. The value of moral action does not depend, at the natural-philosophical level, on an afterlife economy of reward and punishment. The virtuous person is happier, more ordered, more rational, and less disturbed. The vicious person is internally disordered. Ethics is grounded in the structure of human practical reason and affective life.

Pomponazzi also distinguishes speculative and practical perfection. Not everyone can or should live as a speculative philosopher. The human community requires farmers, artisans, rulers, soldiers, householders, and many other practical roles. If everyone pursued pure contemplation, civic life would collapse. Therefore, the universal human end cannot be speculative perfection in the strictest sense; it must include practical virtue accessible to different kinds of people.

This is a philosophy of mind because it assigns different forms of rationality to human life. The practical intellect is not inferior in the sense of being worthless. It is the mode of reason most relevant to the common human good. Speculative intellect is the summit for the few; practical rationality is the shared moral form of human life.

16. Religion, social cognition, and “useful fables”

Pomponazzi’s treatment of religious belief is provocative but more nuanced than simple cynicism. He argues that political and religious legislators often teach immortality, rewards, and punishments because such doctrines help guide people toward moral conduct. Since human beings differ in rational capacity and moral disposition, some are moved by virtue itself, while others need fear, hope, images, stories, and sanctions.

He compares this to therapeutic practice. A physician or nurse may use simplified or even mythic representations to guide a patient toward health. Likewise, religious and political myths can function as moral medicine for the soul. They are not to be assessed only by the standards of demonstrative natural philosophy; they belong also to pedagogy, politics, imagination, and collective moral formation.

This yields a sophisticated account of social mind. Human cognition is unequal, affect-laden, image-dependent, and socially governable. Beliefs are not merely private representations; they organize conduct, communities, and institutions. Religion operates partly through imagination, fear, hope, ritual, authority, and symbolic narratives. Pomponazzi does not reduce all religion to fraud. He treats it as part of the providential and civic ordering of human beings, while still insisting that natural philosophy must distinguish demonstrative truth from politically or morally useful representation.

17. Miracles and the limits of naturalization

It is important not to overstate Pomponazzi’s naturalism. He is willing to naturalize many reports of magic, prophecy, prodigies, fascination, occult healing, and demonic action. But he does not simply declare every miracle impossible. SEP notes that he does not reduce certain biblical miracles, such as the raising of Lazarus, to natural causes; and with cases like Catherine of Siena or Francis of Assisi, he may explore natural possibilities while still allowing a pious miraculous interpretation.

This matters for the philosophy of mind because it shows the boundary of his project. He does not claim that natural philosophy exhausts all reality. He claims that, when acting as natural philosophers, we should not appeal to supernatural minds or demons where natural causes suffice. Theological judgment remains a different order of discourse.

18. Non-English reception and interpretive lines

The non-English scholarship is especially helpful because it often frames Pomponazzi less as an isolated “denier of immortality” and more as a theorist of mind, cognition, and graded being.

Italian discussions emphasize the psycho-physical unity of the human being, the continuity of nature, and the human soul as a corporeal form at the limit of immateriality. The Treccani article stresses that Pomponazzi rejects both Averroist and Thomist “spiritualist” readings and instead locates the intellect within a hierarchy in which human beings are above animals but below separated intelligences.

Vittoria Perrone Compagni’s Italian essay, titled “Mens, intellectus, ratio: scala dell’essere e modi di conoscenza in Pietro Pomponazzi,” is especially relevant because its very framing treats Pomponazzi through “mind,” “intellect,” “reason,” the scale of being, and modes of knowledge. That is close to exactly the project of extracting a philosophy of mind from his work.

Spanish scholarship, especially García-Valverde, is valuable on the agent intellect. It highlights the tension between Pomponazzi’s anthropological materialism and his need for an external or separated principle of abstraction. This line of interpretation makes clear that the agent intellect is not a minor scholastic technicality; it is the pressure point where embodied human cognition touches the cosmic order of separated mind.

French scholarship usefully stresses method and naturalization. Thierry Gontier’s discussion emphasizes that De immortalitate is framed by the question of what can be said when revelations and miracles are bracketed within strict natural limits. Other French work on De incantationibus highlights Pomponazzi’s effort to produce a naturalistic etiology of marvelous effects without collapsing his whole worldview into crude skepticism.

German reception is also significant. The Meiner bilingual Latin-German edition frames the first chapter around the thesis that the human being has a double nature and occupies a middle position between the mortal and the immortal. German scholarship on Pomponazzi and the “immortality of virtue” also reinforces the ethical dimension: if the soul’s natural immortality is unavailable, virtue itself becomes the locus of permanence and value.

19. Systematic reconstruction: Pomponazzi’s philosophy of mind

Extracted systematically, Pomponazzi’s philosophy of mind contains the following theses.

First, mind is a power of soul, and soul is the form of a living body. Human mentality is not an independent substance added to a body, but the rational dimension of an ensouled organism.

Second, human cognition is phantasm-dependent. There is no natural human intellection without images, memory, sensory traces, linguistic signs, or imaginative representations. This is the decisive argument against natural proof of individual immortality.

Third, human intellect is formally higher than sensation but materially conditioned by it. The intellect does not use a bodily organ in the same way the eye sees or the ear hears, but it depends on bodily cognition for its objects.

Fourth, human mind is intermediate. It is higher than animal cognition but lower than divine or celestial intellect. Its dignity is real, but qualified.

Fifth, the agent intellect is externalized or at least problematically separated. Pomponazzi needs a principle of abstraction, but he resists making that principle an immortal component of the human soul. This creates a productive instability in his system.

Sixth, there are genuinely separated minds, but they are not human minds. God and the celestial intelligences represent non-phantasmatic intellect. They are not distractions from the psychology; they are the upper boundary that defines what human cognition lacks.

Seventh, angelic and demonic minds are treated as serious theoretical possibilities but restricted by epistemological and causal constraints. If they are immaterial, their knowledge of particulars and direct bodily causation become problematic; if they are sensory and corporeal, they cease to be purely separated spirits.

Eighth, imagination has causal force. It affects the body through passions, spirits, humors, and temperament. Pomponazzi’s psychology is psychosomatic, not merely representational.

Ninth, will is embedded in causal order. Human volition is not an unconditioned faculty. It is shaped by intellect, appetite, imagination, temperament, celestial influence, and divine causality. Freedom, where preserved, is limited and often construed as suspension or withholding rather than absolute spontaneity.

Tenth, ethics is grounded in practical rationality, not natural proof of afterlife. Virtue is its own essential reward; vice is its own essential punishment. The moral life depends on the structure of human mind and affect, not merely on eschatological expectation.

Eleventh, religious belief has a social-psychological function. Myths of immortality, reward, and punishment can guide the many toward virtue. This is not merely contempt for religion; it is an account of how image-dependent, affective, socially embedded minds are governed.

Twelfth, the philosophy of mind is cosmological. Human mind cannot be understood in isolation from the order of nature, celestial causality, divine providence, and the hierarchy of forms. Pomponazzi’s naturalism is not secular mechanism; it is Aristotelian-cosmic naturalism.

20. Main tensions and unresolved problems

Pomponazzi’s system is powerful because it is disciplined, but it is not free of tensions.

The first tension concerns abstraction. If the agent intellect is not part of the human soul, how exactly do human beings form universal concepts? Pomponazzi’s appeal to external illumination or application to the agent intellect explains why abstraction is possible only by invoking a principle whose relation to the human mind remains obscure.

The second tension concerns the phrase “immortal in a qualified sense.” If the human soul is materially dependent, what exactly is the positive content of its qualified immortality? Sometimes this means participation in universal intelligibility; sometimes it means proximity to separated forms; sometimes it means the dignity of rational activity. But it does not mean individual survival on natural grounds.

The third tension concerns faith and philosophy. Pomponazzi professes submission to Christian doctrine while arguing that natural philosophy cannot prove, and may even disfavor, individual immortality. Interpreters differ on whether this is sincere theological humility, strategic caution, radical fideism, or a deliberately destabilizing performance. SEP notes the interpretive difficulty created by his irony, reversals, and repeated appeals to the distinction between natural philosophy and theology.

The fourth tension concerns determinism. De fato moves between strong necessity and theological attempts to preserve contingency. This affects the philosophy of mind directly because the will’s status remains unsettled: is practical reason genuinely free, or merely part of the causal chain known perfectly only to God?

The fifth tension concerns the status of religious and moral fictions. If immortality is politically useful but naturally indemonstrable, how should philosophers relate to civic religion? Pomponazzi’s answer is subtle but dangerous: myths may be morally necessary for many people, while philosophers must distinguish their practical function from their demonstrative truth.

21. Final synthesis

Pomponazzi’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one sentence: the human mind is the rational power of an embodied animal, capable of universality but incapable, by nature, of exercising intellect apart from sensation, imagination, and the living body; above it stand genuinely separated intellects—celestial intelligences and God—whose non-phantasmatic cognition defines the limit human beings approach but do not naturally cross.

That makes his work unusually valuable for philosophy of mind. He gives us an account of embodied cognition, imagination, affect, practical reason, social belief, determinism, and the hierarchy of minds. He also forces a sharp distinction between three things that are often conflated: having intellect, having immaterial intellectual content, and being an individually immortal intellectual substance.

For Pomponazzi, human beings have intellect. They grasp universals. They deliberate, moralize, speculate, and participate in the divine order. But they do so as living bodies, through phantasms, in time, with unequal capacities, under natural and celestial conditions, and within a providential cosmos whose highest mind is God. The human mind is therefore neither merely animal nor properly angelic. It is the fragile middle: rational, embodied, luminous, dependent, and mortal within nature.

PreviousMarsilio Ficino NextFrancisco Suárez

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
